agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver Buildings Seismic Risk Reduction Action Plan
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote June 2, 2026 on a 5-year Buildings Seismic Risk Reduction Action Plan targeting older buildings. The plan focuses on concrete residential/commercial towers, wood apartments, unreinforced masonry residential buildings, and small commercial buildings. Over 65% of citywide risk is concentrated in six neighbourhoods. A magnitude 7.2 earthquake could cause 1,370 severe injuries/fatalities, displace 365,340 people, and cause $17B in direct losses in Vancouver.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for council decision and public hearing dates — this could affect your building's compliance requirements.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on Vancouver's Vision Zero Safe Mobility Plan
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council will vote on June 2, 2026 to approve the Vision Zero Safe Mobility Plan, which sets a target to reduce traffic fatalities and serious injuries by at least 5% annually until zero is reached before 2050. The plan includes near-term actions over the next five years, such as lower speed limits on arterial and collector streets, redesigned intersections, and more bike lanes. Between 2015 and 2024, over 130 people died and more than 90,000 were injured on Vancouver streets.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on June 2, 2026 — this could affect your delivery routes, parking, or customer access.

agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote June 2, 2026 on proposed amendments to Vancouver's Election By-law. Changes include: allowing election staff to review and process mail ballot applications before the 18th day before general voting day (ballots still issued only after ballot form is finalized); permitting voters who need an assisted voting device to cast their ballot at the Election Office; and updating the City Clerk's disclosure statement posting method. If approved, these rules apply to the 2026 municipal election.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Metro West business licence expansion to include Langley
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council will vote on June 2, 2026 on adding the Township of Langley to the Metro West Inter-Municipal Business Licence (IMBL) programme. If all seven partner councils approve by October 15, 2026, the expanded IMBL takes effect January 1, 2027. Eligible businesses are construction, building repair/maintenance, and in-home health care professionals. Existing IMBL holders renewing for 2027 will automatically be authorized to operate in Langley; no additional application needed. The IMBL fee is $300.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for Vancouver council's decision on June 2, 2026 — if approved, no action needed for existing IMBL holders; new applicants can apply for the expanded licence starting January 1, 2027.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver Empty Homes Tax rule changes up for council vote
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council will vote June 2, 2026 on expanding the Vacant New Inventory exemption under the Empty Homes Tax (EHT) from developments of 5+ units to include developments of 2+ units (duplexes and multiplexes). Currently, only projects with 5+ residential properties qualify; this change would exempt unsold new units in smaller multi-unit projects from the 3% tax on assessed value until sold or occupied. Vacant New Inventory declarations rose from 261 in 2024 to 597 in 2025.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you own or develop a duplex or multiplex in Vancouver, watch for the council vote on June 2, 2026 — approval would reduce your EHT exposure on unsold new units.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver council to fast-track rental housing in residential zones
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has enacted amendments to the Secured Rental Policy and related bylaws, effective June 2, 2026. The changes permit 6-storey 100% market rental developments in low-density transition areas (East and West Areas). Projects in the West Area must include a minimum 20% of residential floor area as below-market units, with starting rents set at 10% below CMHC averages for 100% residential buildings or 20% below for mixed-use buildings. Rent escalation between tenancies may be re-indexed to current CMHC averages at the same discount rate.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Review the updated Secured Rental Policy and eligibility maps (East/West Areas) to determine if your property qualifies for 6-storey rental development under the new RR-3C or RR-3B district schedules.

minutes_briefs.title: Council voted to take no action against Mayor Sim over harassment complaint
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council voted to receive an Integrity Commissioner report and impose no sanctions on Mayor Sim. An amendment that would have required Mayor Sim to apologize to Councillor Orr for harassing comments and a social media post was defeated. Council then voted to accept the original motion to take no action. The decision is final.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
MOVED by Councillor Montague
SECONDED by Councillor Klassen

That Council imposes no sanctions and receives the report for information.

carried

AMENDMENT MOVED by Councillor Fry
SECONDED by Councillor Bligh

THAT the motion be deleted and replaced with the following:

THAT Council receive the Integrity Commissioner Investigation Report;

FURTHER THAT Council direct that Mayor Sim issues a letter of apology to Councillor Orr for:

•
misusing the influence of his office and for his comments at the           
April 8, 2025 Press Conference, which were harassing of Councillor Orr as they suggested he would incite violence and was antisemitic; and

•
for posting the October 4, 2025 social media post which was a continuation of that harassment, by suggesting Councillor Orr supported terrorism or antisemitism;

AND FURTHER THAT If an apology is not forthcoming within 14 days of Council directing the apology to be made, Council issue a letter of reprimand to Mayor Sim reprimanding him for:

•
misusing the influence of his office and for his comments at the April 8, 2025 Press Conference, which were harassing of Councillor Orr as they suggested he would incite violence and was antisemitic;

•
for posting the October 4, 2025 social media post which was a continuation of that harassment, by suggesting Councillor Orr supported terrorism or antisemitism.

LOST (Vote No. 11675)
(Councillors Dominato, Kirby-Yung, Klassen, Meiszner, Montague and Zhou opposed) 
(Mayor Sim absent for the vote due to a conflict of interest)

The amendment having lost, the motion was put and CARRIED (Vote No. 11676) with Councillors Bligh, Fry, Maloney and Orr opposed, and Mayor Sim absent for the vote due to a conflict of interest.

minutes_briefs.title: Council adopted Vancouver's Buildings Seismic Risk Reduction Action Plan
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council unanimously approved the Buildings Seismic Risk Reduction Action Plan. The plan directs staff to look at successful programs in other West Coast cities, like San Francisco's soft-story retrofit programs, and to find ways to protect affordable housing and heritage buildings while making them safer during earthquakes. Council voted to have staff report back on how the plan is being put into action.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you own a vulnerable building (e.g., soft-story or unreinforced masonry), start planning for potential retrofit requirements and look into preservation tools like financing or permitting.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
FINAL MOTION AS APPROVED
THAT Council adopt the Buildings Seismic Risk Reduction Action Plan described within the Report dated May 5, 2026, entitled “Buildings Seismic Risk Reduction Action Plan”, and direct staff to report back on its implementation;
FURTHER THAT staff, in implementing and reporting back on the Plan, look to examples in other West Coast cities (including but not limited to San Francisco’s PASS, PACE and Soft Storey retrofit programs) and consider opportunities to more robustly integrate affordable housing retention and heritage conservation objectives, through targeted preservation tools like financing, permitting and by-laws, including measures to minimize displacement and support the preservation of existing rental, character buildings and built form, while advancing seismic safety.

CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11679) with Councillors Bligh and Montague absent for the vote.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved Vision Zero Safe Mobility Plan, no major street speed cuts
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council voted to approve the Vision Zero Safe Mobility Plan, which aims to eliminate traffic deaths and serious injuries. However, council also voted to remove a key action that would have reduced speed limits on major streets to 30 or 40 km/h. Council directed staff to focus on safety while also keeping traffic moving for people, goods, transit, and emergency services. This means businesses that rely on major roads for deliveries, customer access, or employee commutes will not face new speed restrictions on those routes.
minutes_briefs.action_required: No immediate action required — monitor future Vision Zero safety measures that may affect your delivery routes or parking.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved expanding Empty Homes Tax exemption to 2+ unit developments
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver Council voted unanimously to expand the Empty Homes Tax (also called the Vacancy Tax) exemption for new buildings. Previously, only new residential developments with 5 or more units could get this exemption. Now, new developments with 2 or more units will also qualify. This change is meant to encourage more housing construction by reducing the tax burden on smaller new projects. Council also directed the Mayor to ask the Province to make a similar change to the provincial Speculation and Vacancy Tax, so the rules are consistent and don't create extra barriers for builders.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you own a new 2-4 unit residential development in Vancouver, check if you now qualify for the Empty Homes Tax exemption.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
MOVED by Councillor Klassen
SECONDED by Councillor Zhou
A. THAT Council approve, in principle, an amendment to Vacancy Tax By-law to expand eligibility for the Vacant New Inventory exemption, currently limited to residential properties that are part of a residential development of 5 or more residential properties, to include residential properties that are part of a residential development of 2 or more residential properties;
FURTHER THAT the Director of Legal Services, be instructed to bring forward for enactment a by-law in accordance with this decision.
B. THAT Council request that the Mayor write a letter to the Province to seek amendments to the Speculation and Vacancy Tax Act to provide for a comparable update to the Province’s Vacant New Inventory exemption in order to ensure consistent treatment, reduce unintended barriers to housing delivery, and support shared housing supply objectives.

CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11738) (Councillor Fry and Mayor Sim absent for the vote)

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved rezoning for 100 rental units at West 4th and Alma
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver Council unanimously approved a rezoning application for 3731-3743 West 4th Avenue and 1975 Alma Street. The properties will be rezoned to a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) district, which is a custom zoning category that allows for a specific development plan. This change permits the construction of a six-storey mixed-use building with 100 rental housing units and ground-floor commercial space. The maximum floor space ratio (the ratio of a building's total floor area to the size of the lot) was increased to 3.9, and the maximum building height was raised to 24 metres (about 79 feet). The approval is subject to conditions, including a Housing Agreement that will secure the rental units for the long term.
minutes_briefs.action_required: No immediate action required — the rezoning is approved, but the developer must still meet conditions before construction can begin.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved 12-storey mixed-use rezoning at 1827 West 5th Ave
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver Council unanimously approved rezoning 1827 West 5th Avenue from residential and commercial zones to a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) district. This allows a 12-storey mixed-use building with ground-floor commercial space and 37 strata-titled residential units. The new rules increase the maximum floor space ratio (the total floor area compared to the lot size) from 2.70 to 4.5, and raise the maximum building height from 23 metres (75 feet) to 40.7 metres (134 feet). The Sign By-law and Noise Control By-law were also amended to match the new zone.

minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council approved a rezoning application for 1030 and 1056 Burnaby Street. The decision allows a new six-storey building with 90 rental units, including 20% below-market-rate units. The existing 215 rental units on the site will now be permanently secured as market rental housing. The rezoning changes the land from RM-5A (Residential) to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) District, allowing taller buildings up to 134 feet.

minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver Council unanimously approved a rezoning application for 4338 and 4362 Cambie Street. This permits a 12-storey mixed-use building with 101 strata-titled residential units (condos) and ground-floor commercial space. minutes_briefs.title: Council approved 24-storey rental tower at 1188 Cardero Street
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver Council has approved a rezoning for 1188 Cardero Street, allowing a 24-storey rental residential building. The property was rezoned from RM-5D (Residential) to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) District. This increases the maximum floor space ratio from 7.0 to 8.83 and the maximum building height from 58 metres (190 feet) to 75 metres (246 feet). The developer must also enter into a Housing Agreement with the City, which will be finalized before the new zoning bylaw takes effect. The approval is conditional on the developer meeting all requirements listed in the report.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved rezoning for 18-storey rental at 360 East 14th Ave
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver Council approved the rezoning of 360 East 14th Avenue from R3-3 (Residential) to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) District. This allows an 18-storey rental building with a floor space ratio (FSR) of 6.5 and a height of 56.9 metres. At least 20% of the residential floor area must be below-market rental units. The approval is subject to conditions, including a Housing Agreement to secure affordability. The decision is final.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved two rental towers at Station Street and Terminal Ave
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council approved a rezoning application for 1220 Station Street and 201-213 Terminal Avenue. The property will be rezoned from FC-2 (Commercial) to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) District. This allows construction of two rental towers — a 28-storey west tower and a 36-storey east tower connected by an eight-storey podium. The project will include 470 rental units, with 20% of the residential floor area set aside as below-market housing. The development also includes commercial and office space. Council also approved changes to the Sign By-law and Noise Control By-law to match the new zoning.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved rezoning for 572 rental units on Great Northern Way
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver Council approved a rezoning application for 455-485 Great Northern Way, a portion of 565 Great Northern Way, and 1850 Thornton Avenue. The new zoning allows a mixed-use development with a 20-storey office building, two 36-storey residential towers, and 572 rental housing units. At least 20% of the residential space must be below-market rental units. The project also includes a 90-space childcare centre that will be owned by the City, and a 0.49-hectare public open space. The developer must meet all conditions listed in the report before the new zoning bylaw takes effect.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved rezoning of 1500-1580 West 3rd Ave for a 17-storey hotel
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council voted to rezone 1500-1580 West 3rd Avenue from industrial (IC-2) to Comprehensive Development (CD-1) district. This allows a 17-storey building with 160 hotel units, a maximum floor space ratio of 16.4 (up from 3.0), and a maximum height of 53.6 metres (176 feet, up from 30.5 metres). The rezoning also includes amendments to the Sign By-law and Noise Control By-law for the new CD-1 district. The decision was carried with one councillor opposed and three absent.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you own or operate a business near this site, review the new CD-1 bylaw and sign/noise regulations once enacted to understand any impacts on your operations.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved rezoning for Jewish Community Centre towers in Vancouver
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver Council voted to refer a rezoning application for the Jewish Community Centre at 940-950 West 41st Avenue to a Public Hearing. The plan would allow two rental towers with 630 units (at least 20% non-profit), a private school, a childcare, and a replacement community centre. The bylaw changes would increase the maximum floor space ratio from 4.49 to 5.77 and the maximum building height from 88 metres to 136 metres. Council approved the recommendations in principle, and the Director of Legal Services will prepare the necessary bylaw amendments for the Public Hearing.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Attend or submit comments at the Public Hearing to voice support or concerns about the proposed rezoning and development.

The plan would change the land-use rules to allow four residential towers with 863 secured rental apartments, ground-floor shops, a social service centre, and a childcare facility. minutes_briefs.title: Council approved VGH expansion: taller building, more hospital space
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver Council has approved a zoning change for Vancouver General Hospital. The decision allows a new 11-storey institutional building with hospital uses at 818-990 West 10th Avenue and 803-899 West 12th Avenue, plus a temporary cycling centre at 2785 Ash Street. The bylaw amendment increases the maximum floor space ratio (the amount of floor area allowed relative to the land size) from 2.60 to 2.65, increases maximum site coverage from 55% to 60%, and updates parking requirements. The approval is subject to conditions in the city report, and the city is not obligated to finalize the rezoning until all conditions are met.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved 23-storey rezoning referral for Yukon Street site
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver Council voted to refer a rezoning application for 6333-6369 Yukon Street to a Public Hearing. The plan would change the land use from Residential Mid-Rise to Residential High-Rise and rezone the property from R1-1 (single-family residential) to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development, a custom zoning for large projects). This would allow a 23-storey residential building with a floor space ratio (FSR, the ratio of total floor area to lot size) of 6.55 and a height of 70 metres (230 feet), up from the current 0.6 FSR and 11.5 metres. The project also includes a Housing Agreement to secure affordable units. Council adopted the referral on consent, meaning it passed without debate.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved rezoning of former Molson Brewery site to mixed-use high-rise
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver Council voted to move forward with changing the land-use rules for the former Molson Brewery site at 1550 Burrard Street and 1655 West 1st Avenue. The site will be rezoned from industrial to a mixed-use high-rise designation. Council also approved guiding principles for future development of the site. The decision is conditional on Metro Vancouver agreeing to change the regional land-use designation from 'Industrial' to 'General Urban'. The amendment bylaw will go to a Public Hearing before final approval.
minutes_briefs.action_required: No immediate action required — monitor the Public Hearing date for the rezoning bylaw.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved Vancouver Villages Plan and zoning changes for public hearing
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver Council voted to refer the Villages Plan and related zoning amendments to a public hearing. The plan updates land use designations, creates new zoning districts (R3-4, C-2D, C-2E), and repeals the RM-12 district. It also amends several other bylaws including the Sign, Noise, Parking, and Subdivision bylaws. Council approved these changes in principle, meaning they are not final yet — the public hearing will give residents and business owners a chance to comment before Council makes a final decision.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Attend or submit comments at the upcoming public hearing to voice how these zoning changes could affect your property or business.
